root_cause: Connection-pool exhaustion in pool-svc. The service was started with SLOW_QUERY_DISABLED unset, so every /order request executed a SLOW_SECONDS=5 'SELECT pg_sleep(5)' that holds its pool connection for the full 5 seconds. With POOL_SIZE=5 and the load generator driving 12 concurrent /order workers (concurrency > pool size), all 5 pooled connections were perpetually checked out by these slow queries. The health check (/healthz) and new order requests could never acquire a connection, so they blocked and timed out ('healthz: could not acquire connection from pool: context canceled'). Postgres itself was healthy and near-idle (pg_sleep burns no CPU) and the payments neighbor was unaffected because it runs the same image with SLOW_QUERY_DISABLED=1 and fast queries — confirming the DB-overload hypothesis was a red herring and the fault was purely the slow-query pool-holding configuration on pool-svc.
actions_taken: 1) Triaged: docker ps showed all containers up; pool-svc logs showed repeated 'healthz: could not acquire connection from pool: context canceled' and startup banner 'slow query ENABLED (slow queries hold connections), pool_max=5'. 2) Queried pg_stat_activity and found exactly 5 active 'SELECT pg_sleep(5)' queries (Timeout/PgSleep) occupying the whole pool, with Postgres otherwise idle. 3) Reviewed the compose file and confirmed pool-svc had SLOW_QUERY_DISABLED intentionally unset while the stable payments neighbor set SLOW_QUERY_DISABLED=1. 4) Confirmed the outage: /healthz timed out on 3/3 probes. 5) Applied the least-risky fix: added SLOW_QUERY_DISABLED=1 to the pool-svc service environment in docker-compose.yaml (matching the known-good payments config) and recreated ONLY pool-svc via 'docker compose up -d pool-svc'. 6) Verified recovery: pool-svc now logs 'slow query disabled (fast queries)'; /healthz returns 'ok' on 5/5 probes; /order returns {"ok":true,"query":"fast"}; no pg_sleep queries remain in pg_stat_activity. 7) Confirmed the payments neighbor was left untouched and still healthy. No destructive actions and no changes to Postgres or the payments/load containers.
postmortem: Impact: pool-svc health checks and user order placement timed out while the service and database stayed running. Trigger: pool-svc ran with slow (5s) queries that hold a pooled connection for their entire duration, combined with POOL_SIZE=5 and an offered load of 12 concurrent requests. Once concurrent slow requests exceeded the pool size, every connection stayed checked out and the pool starved — health checks and new work could not get a connection, producing timeouts even though CPU and DB were idle. This is a classic connection-pool saturation / head-of-line-blocking failure, not resource exhaustion. Resolution: disabling the connection-holding slow-query behavior (SLOW_QUERY_DISABLED=1, matching the stable payments neighbor) freed the pool immediately and restored service. Blameless lessons: (a) the pool was undersized relative to worst-case concurrency and had no per-query timeout to bound how long a connection can be held; (b) the health check shared the same exhaustible pool as request traffic, so a saturated pool made the service look dead — a lightweight/dedicated liveness path would have degraded more gracefully. Follow-ups: bound query execution time (statement_timeout / context deadline) so slow work cannot pin connections indefinitely; size the pool for expected concurrency or add backpressure/queue limits and load shedding when the pool is exhausted; isolate health checks from the request connection pool; and add alerting on pool-wait time and checked-out-connection saturation so this is caught before user-facing timeouts.
